summary: Android-style ESP32 Footstep Power Generator web app that connects to an ESP32 device over WiFi, displays real-time power generation data (steps, power, battery, voltage, current, energy), shows live-updating charts, features a dark futuristic UI with blue/cyan/green gradients, and persists lifetime data in browser storage.
architectural_notes: Frontend-only web app (React + TypeScript); no Motoko backend needed — ESP32 is the data source
ESP32 JSON endpoint: { steps, power, battery, totalEnergy, usedEnergy, voltage, current } polled via HTTP GET every 1 second
Charts implemented using Chart.js or Recharts (MPAndroidChart not available in web)
Lifetime data persisted in localStorage (totalFootsteps, totalEnergyGenerated, totalRuntime)
known_issues: Browser CORS policy may block direct HTTP requests to ESP32; ESP32 firmware must include CORS headers
imageRequirements:
## Required Images
- Futuristic glowing foot/footstep icon on dark background with cyan and green neon glow effect (foot-icon.dim_128x128.png)
- Animated-style lightning bolt power icon with blue and cyan gradient neon glow on dark background (power-icon.dim_128x128.png)
- Futuristic battery icon with green gradient fill and neon glow outline on dark background (battery-icon.dim_128x128.png)

existing_features.title: Build a WiFi connectivity service in the frontend that polls a configurable ESP32 IP address (default: http://192.168.4.1/data) via HTTP GET every 1 second. Parse the JSON response containing steps, power, battery, totalEnergy, usedEnergy, voltage, and current fields. Implement auto-reconnect logic with exponential backoff on failure. Track connection state as ONLINE or OFFLINE.

Build a Dashboard screen that displays: footstep count, current power in Watts, battery percentage with charging animation, voltage, current, total energy generated, total energy used, and a prominent ONLINE/OFFLINE system status indicator. Animate a foot icon whenever a new step is detected. Include a power flow animation when power > 0.

Build a Graph screen with four live-updating line charts: Power vs Time, Steps vs Time, Battery vs Time, and Energy vs Time. Each chart maintains a rolling window of the last 60 data points. Use a charting library (Recharts or Chart.js via react-chartjs-2) suitable for React.

Persist lifetime stats (total footsteps, total energy generated, total runtime) in localStorage as a fallback for offline use, and sync increments to the backend canister when connected. On app load, merge localStorage values with backend values taking the maximum of each field.

Apply a dark futuristic UI theme with a near-black background, gradient accents in blue (#00BFFF), cyan (#00FFFF), and green (#39FF14), large bold numbers for key metrics, a glowing/neon card style, and a bottom navigation bar for switching between Dashboard and Graph screens. Ensure the layout is fully mobile-optimized.

Add a Settings panel accessible from the header where the user can view and change the ESP32 IP address/URL. The new URL should be saved to localStorage and take effect immediately on the next poll cycle.

new_feature_requests.summary: WiFi connectivity to ESP32 at configurable IP (default http://192.168.4.1/data), HTTP GET polling every 1 second, auto-reconnect, connection status indicator
Dashboard screen showing footstep count, current power (W), battery %, voltage, current, total energy generated, total energy used, and ONLINE/OFFLINE status
Graph screen with live-updating charts: Power vs Time, Steps vs Time, Battery vs Time, Energy vs Time
Animation system: foot icon animation on step detection, battery charging animation, power flow animation, smooth UI transitions
Persistent data storage using localStorage: total footsteps, total energy generated, total runtime — survives app restart
Dark futuristic UI with gradient colors (blue, cyan, green), large readable numbers, mobile-optimized layout
Error handling: show 'Device Offline' message when ESP32 unreachable, auto-retry connection

Create a backend actor that stores lifetime persistent data: total footsteps, total energy generated (Wh), and total runtime (seconds). Expose query and update methods to read and increment these values.
